data.title: Eli Lilly unveils long-term data on Omvoh for Crohn's disease
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (LLY) said that data from a phase 3 open-label extension study indicated that its IL-23 antagonist Omvoh (mirikizumab) led to steroid-free remission over three years.
  • Data from the VIVID-2 study found that at 152 weeks, corticosteroid-free clinical remission was 91.2%. Also, 82.1% of patients showed a  ≥3-point reduction on the Urgency Numeric Rating Scale (UNRS).
  • In addition, 71.7% of patients saw a UNRS ≤2.

data.title: Wall Street drifts lower after Walmart earnings and further economic data
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages shifted lower on Thursday as investors digested Walmart’s (WMT) latest earnings print and additional economic data.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last -0.6%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.6%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.8%.

From a sector vantage point, four of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with utilities leading the way. At the other end, financials have suffered the most.

Treasury yields ticked down as the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved lower by 1 basis point to 3.45%, and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 1 basis point to 4.06%.

On the economic front, the Philly Fed Manufacturing Index rose in February. The U.S. trade deficit swelled more than expected in December, and the initial jobless claims fell more than expected in the past week.

Federal Reserve speakers, including Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman, Minneapolis Fed Bank President Neel Kashkari, and Chicago Fed Bank President Austan Goolsbee, will speak at different events.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Deere & Company (DE) climbed 12.9%, and shares of EPAM Systems (EPAM) came down by 20.9%.

data.title: Oceaneering projects $390M–$440M EBITDA for 2026 as ADTech drives multiyear growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oceaneering International, Inc. (OII) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Roderick Larson, President and CEO, opened by stating, "We closed out 2025 with strong execution across the business, making continued progress against our strategic priorities." He emphasized continued pricing progression, operational delivery, and growing contributions from Aerospace and Defense Technologies (ADTech), highlighting "our cash balance increasing to $689 million at year-end, further strengthening our financial flexibility."
  • Larson reported 2025 order intake of $3.7 billion, a book-to-bill ratio of 1.33, 140 basis points EBITDA margin expansion, 99% ROV uptime for the second consecutive year, and a 7% improvement in ROV pricing. He also noted the company won its highest ever initial contract award through ADTech and repurchased approximately 1.8 million shares for $40 million.
  • Larson stated, "We reported net income of $178 million or $1.76 per share, a 217% increase year-over-year. This improvement was largely due to a $156 million discrete tax benefit related to the release of U.S. valuation allowances."
  • Michael Sumruld, Senior VP & CFO, noted, "The balance sheet strength and the growth in cash over the last several years...just gives us the opportunity, more flexibility and opportunity to do more when the time is right."

Outlook

  • Larson outlined, "Based on our current backlog, anticipated order intake and market fundamentals, we project consolidated revenue in 2026 to grow in the low to mid-single-digit percentage range. Year-over-year, ADTech revenue will improve significantly. SSR and IMDS revenue improvement will largely offset anticipated declines in OPG and Manufactured Products."
  • The company projects 2026 EBITDA between $390 million and $440 million, with year-over-year improvements in all segments except OPG.
  • Free cash flow for 2026 is anticipated to be $100 million to $120 million, with a year-over-year reduction primarily reflecting early receipt of $37 million in customer payments in Q4 2025 that were originally scheduled for Q1 2026.
  • Organic capital expenditures for 2026 are forecasted between $105 million and $115 million, with 40% allocated to growth and 60% to maintenance.
  • Segment guidance includes: SSR revenue growth in the low to mid-single-digit range and mid-30% EBITDA margins; ROV service mix of 65% drill support and 35% vessel services; Manufactured Products operating income margin in the mid-teens; OPG revenue and operating income to decrease significantly; IMDS operating income margin to improve to the mid-single-digit range; and ADTech operating income margins expected to average in the low teens.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated Q4 2025 revenue was $669 million, a 6% decline year-over-year, primarily due to lower energy-focused business revenue. Operating income was $65.4 million, also declining year-over-year, with increases in ADTech, Manufactured Products, and SSR partially offsetting lower OPG results.
  • Net income reached $178 million or $1.76 per share, driven by a $156 million discrete tax benefit. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $90.5 million, at the high end of guidance but still lower year-over-year.
  • Free cash flow for Q4 was $191 million, benefiting from early customer payments. The year-end cash balance was $689 million, a 38% increase from the end of 2024.
  • SSR operating income increased 7% to $67.8 million, with EBITDA margins rising to 38%. ROV revenue per day utilized rose 7% to $11,210, with a Q4 exit rate of $11,550. ROV fleet utilization declined to 62%.
  • Manufactured Products revenue decreased 7% to $132 million, but operating income rose to $20.4 million, with a margin of 15%. Year-end backlog was $511 million, a 15% drop from 2024.
  • OPG revenue dropped 29% to $131 million, with operating income and margin also declining. IMDS revenue and operating income both declined, while ADTech posted a 43% increase in operating income on 29% higher revenue.

Q&A

  • Keith Beckmann, Pickering Energy Partners LP, asked about government services lead times for ADTech projects. Larson responded that the timing "varies quite a bit," with some projects ramping quickly and others requiring longer engineering and prototyping phases, describing the current mix as both.
  • Beckmann also inquired how other segments supplement ADTech growth. Larson explained their offshore, maritime, and space expertise translates across defense and energy, highlighting unique SUBSAFE certifications and capabilities in submarine sustainment and space systems.
  • Joshua Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC, asked about IMDS and digital software expansion beyond energy. Larson described the integration of machine vision, machine learning, and AI for enhanced inspection capabilities, noting, "...if we can inspect subsea infrastructure the same way with the laser scanning and the 3D model, we can be deploying that off an OPG vessel with an ROV."
  • Jayne probed into M&A strategy. Larson stated, "I don't think it's going to change my mind on big industry consolidation," but sees potential for "bolt-on" technology acquisitions. Sumruld added the strong balance sheet provides "more flexibility and opportunity to do more when the time is right."
  • Brandon Carnovale, Half Moon Capital, asked about autonomous forklifts. Larson said interest is high, with adoption depending on customer readiness, stating, "We're still encouraged and the list is long."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth in ADTech, digital and software expansion, and technology adoption, with a generally positive and inquisitive tone. Questions were constructive, probing timelines, integration, and strategic focus.
  • Management maintained a confident, detailed, and forward-focused tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing strategic priorities, cash strength, and multi-segment integration. Larson's language was measured but optimistic, while Sumruld highlighted financial flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a positive tone, with a slight increase in focus on technology, M&A potential, and digital initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, with explicit revenue, EBITDA, and segment outlooks, building on the preliminary ranges given in Q3. There is heightened emphasis on ADTech as a primary growth driver, with more detail on backlog and government project mix.
  • Management continues to highlight strong cash generation and financial flexibility, but with additional discussion of technology adoption and potential for bolt-on acquisitions.
  • Analysts' questions in Q4 shifted toward timelines for government contract revenue realization and cross-segment synergies, compared to Q3’s focus on operational upgrades and international market penetration.
  • Key metrics such as EBITDA and cash balances improved quarter-over-quarter, though revenue saw a seasonal decline.
  • The tone of both management and analysts remained positive, with management demonstrating high confidence in growth initiatives, especially in ADTech and digital services.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that the year-over-year reduction in free cash flow for 2026 is due to early receipt of customer payments in Q4 2025, which were originally scheduled for Q1 2026.
  • OPG revenue and operating income are forecasted to decrease significantly in 2026 as project mix shifts away from intervention and installation work.
  • Unallocated expenses are expected to average $50 million per quarter, with increases tied to wage inflation, IT costs, and foreign exchange impacts.
  • IMDS faced a loss in Q4 2025 due to a commercial dispute resolution.

Final Takeaway

Oceaneering closed 2025 with strong financial results, robust cash generation, and notable progress in ADTech, which is set to be the main growth engine in 2026. With a projected EBITDA of $390 million to $440 million, the company expects continued expansion in defense and government markets, stable performance in subsea robotics and digital solutions, and a strategic focus on technology-driven growth. Management emphasizes its strengthened financial flexibility, disciplined capital allocation, and readiness to pursue technology acquisitions that align with evolving market needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Blue Owl Capital move adds to private credit unease
data.description:
Shares of alternative asset managers and business development companies were under pressure Thursday amid a media report that Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) is permanently halting redemptions for its retail-focused private credit fund, a move that raised fresh concern over the risks facing the private credit market.

The Financial Times had reported that Blue Owl's (OWL) OBDC II vehicle will no longer reopen to quarterly withdrawals and instead plans to return capital over time as assets are sold. Meanwhile, Blue Owl agreed to sell roughly $1.4B of debt investments to institutional buyers, underscoring efforts to manage outflows.

During a conference call Thursday, Blue Owl (OWL) management described the FT report as a "complete mischaracterization," stressing that it is not halting redemptions but rather changing the method by which it provides redemptions, according to Briefing.com. Part of the reason private credit broadly has come under scrutiny as of late is the potential mismatch between investor redemption terms and the underlying illiquid loans some of these funds hold.

Blue Owl Capital (OWL) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Here are the holdings of the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), along with their Quant ratings and intraday percentage moves (as of 1:35 p.m. ET):

  • Ares Capital (ARCC), 3.17, -3%
  • Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), 3.11, -1.6%
  • Blackstone Secured lending Funding (BXSL), 3.23, -2.8%
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN), 3.09, -4.2%
  • Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), 3.03, -1%
  • Fs Kkr Capital (FSK), 3.15, -3.3%
  • Hercules Capital (HTGC), 2.91, -2.3%
  • Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX), 2.98, -4%
  • Capital Southwest (CSWC), 3.22, -1.5%
  • Trinity Capital (TRIN), 3.17, -2.1%
  • Prospect Capital (PSEC), 3.14, -3.3%
  • Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), 3.44, -2.5%
  • Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL), 3.03, -1.9%
  • Midcap Financial Investment (MFIC), 3.20, -1.9%
  • Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD), 3.32, -1.8%

data.title: Credo receives Buy rating due to its edge in data centers: Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Credo Technology (CRDO) received a Buy rating from Goldman Sachs as the financial firm initiated coverage on the provider of high-speed, short-range connectivity solutions.

Goldman Sachs also set a $175, 12-month price target on the stock.

Credo shares crept up 1% during afternoon trading on Thursday.

The Buy rating stems from Credo's copper-based Active Electrical Cables (AECs), which are critical in the enormous data center buildout.

"We see a favorable near-term setup for the stock, and our FY26/FY27 EPS estimates are 7%/32% above consensus," said Goldman Sachs analysts, led by James Schneider, in a Thursday investor report. "Longer term, we recognize the legitimate debate around copper vs. optical networking solutions—but we expect copper solutions to remain highly relevant until at least 2032."

"AECs provide better signal integrity at lower cost vs. alternatives for short-range connections, and we expect AECs to continue to proliferate in leading-edge datacenters," he added. "We expect Credo to sustain its competitive advantage in AECs given its vertically-integrated business model and see its use of prior generation manufacturing nodes relative to alternatives delivering better price/performance."

Even though data centers are expected to pivot to more optical connectivity solutions in the long term, Credo is also diversifying into those products as well.

Last week, Credo increased its third-quarter fiscal 2026 revenue to range from $404M to $408M compared to its earlier guidance of $335M to $345M.

Credo plans to release its third-quarter financial results post-market on Monday, March 2. A consensus estimate expects Credo to report adjusted earnings per share of $0.94 and GAAP EPS of $0.66. The estimate calls for revenue of $387.6M.

data.title: China drug licensing deals surge nearly 10-fold in value since 2021
data.description:
Drug licensing deals between Chinese companies and global pharmaceutical companies surged to a record $137.7B last year, indicating a nearly 10-fold increase since 2021, according to China-based data provider PharmCube.

Companies based in the Greater China region, including Hong Kong, Macau, and Taiwan, inked 186 cross-border out-licensing deals worth $137.7B in 2025. In 2021, there were 65 such deals worth nearly $13.9B, Reuters reported, quoting PharmCube.

Licensing deals represent a popular strategy that leading drugmakers deploy to replenish pipelines as their blockbuster drugs face copycats with the expiration of key patents.

The findings underscore how Big Pharma is increasingly turning to China for licensing ahead of a massive revenue deficit tied to several notable patent cliffs.

Industry advisory firm Vision Life Sciences projects a potential $200B hit to annual global pharmaceutical revenue from 2026 to 2030 as some of the best-selling medicines lose patent exclusivities, paving the way for generic or biosimilar competition.

According to Vision Lifesciences, Merck (MRK) and Pfizer (PFE)/Astellas (ALPMF) (ALPMY) are among those facing patent expirations for their diabetes drug, Januvia/Janumet, and cancer drug, Xtandi, respectively.

China has remained a major attraction for international drugmakers seeking licensing arrangements this year, as the deal count has already reached 38, while the total value of those deals has approached $49.0B.

The average deal size so far in 2026 stands at $1.3B, indicating a ~76% rise from 2025 and roughly a six-fold increase from the average in 2021. The increase is largely driven by AstraZeneca’s (AZN) obesity drug deal worth up to $18.5B with China’s CSPC Pharmaceutical (CSPCY) (CHJTF) and AbbVie’s (ABBV) cancer drug deal worth up to $5.6B with RemeGen.

data.title: Snowflake in spotlight as Wells Fargo survey shows uptick in consumption trends
data.description:
Snowflake (SNOW) was in the spotlight on Thursday after Wells Fargo said a recent survey showed an uptick in consumption trends at the data warehousing company.

“Our 4Q survey indicates an uptick in consumption trends among large customers vs. 3Q and improvement in future outlooks,” analyst Ryan MacWilliams wrote in a note to clients. “Views around AI skewed more positive, with qualitative commentary pointing to AI initiatives turning into greater SNOW spend.” MacWilliams has an Overweight rating and a $290 price target on Snowflake.

Delving deeper, some of the highlights of the survey include an uptick in indications for Snowflake-related spending growth of between 10% and 25% next year, which MacWilliams said was “the most positive read in 5 quarters.”

Other highlights include more indications of Snowflake being used for generative artificial intelligence strategies; and continued share gains, as customers who are also using competitor Databricks, intend to boost Snowflake-related spending over the next two years.

“We believe SNOW continues to look positioned to capture greater consumption from new AI workloads and grow within existing customers,” MacWilliams added.

Snowflake is set to report fourth-quarter results after the close of trading on Feb. 25. A consensus of analysts expects the firm to earn an adjusted $0.27 per share on $1.26B in revenue.

data.title: Deere soars to all-time high after beat-and-raise Q1 report, sees 2026 as ag cycle bottom
data.description:
Deere (DE) +13.2% in Thursday's trading to an all-time intraday high of $672.95 after easily topping expectations for FQ1 earnings and revenues, as profits in its construction business more than doubled, and issuing a bullish outlook for full-year sales growth.

The company reported Q1 net income of $656M, or $2.42/share, down from $869M, or $3.19/share, in the year-earlier quarter, but well ahead of the $2.02/share FactSet analyst consensus estimate, as revenues rose 13% Y/Y to $9.61B, sparked by a 34% jump in net sales for its construction and forestry division and 24% growth for its small agriculture and turf segment.

For FY 2026, raised its net income guidance to $4.5B-$5B from its previous outlook of $4B-$4.75B and above the $4.43B analyst consensus, and sees full-year net sales in the construction and forestry and small agriculture and turf segments to rise by ~15% each compared with its earlier forecast for a ~10% increase.

"We're encouraged by the ongoing recovery in demand within both the construction and small agriculture segments," CEO John May said. "These positive developments reinforce our belief that 2026 represents the bottom of the current cycle."

Deere (DE) said dealer inventory is in much better shape, with North American new large agriculture inventories aligned to retail demand, used large ag units down meaningfully from prior peaks, and small ag and turf field inventories running ~40% below last year, setting up cleaner channels and healthier replacement demand as the cycle turns.

While tariffs remain a $1.2B headwind in 2026, Deere (DE) has largely offset them through cost efficiencies and disciplined pricing, leaving the company roughly price-cost neutral for the year and preserving margin leverage if tariff relief or further mix improvement materialize.

Oppenheimer analyst Kristen Owen said Deere (DE) ended the quarter with relatively lean inventories, building historically less stock in Q4 and Q1 and leaving room for potential upside as inventory normalizes through the year.

data.title: Evergy outlines 6% to 8% plus annual EPS growth through 2030 as data center deals fuel transformative demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Evergy (EVRG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Campbell stated the company is "raising our long-term adjusted EPS growth target to 6% to 8% plus through 2030 off of our 2026 guidance midpoint of $4.24 per share." Campbell highlighted that EPS growth is expected to "exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 and through 2030." He noted the outlook is supported by "the recent execution of electric service agreements for 4 data center projects."
  • Campbell reported $2.8 billion invested in infrastructure in 2025 to modernize the grid and replace aging equipment, while acknowledging "our financial results in 2025 were negatively impacted by weather and weak industrial demand throughout the year."
  • He announced the approval of large load power service (LLPS) tariffs in both Kansas and Missouri, establishing "a framework under which new large customers will pay a premium demand rate to locate in our service territories." The company received regulatory approvals for three new natural gas facilities and three solar farms totaling nearly 2,200 megawatts.
  • Campbell revealed new electric service agreements for "4 major data center projects," representing "1.9 gigawatts of steady-state peak demand" and nearly 20% increase in total peak system demand. He added, "We’ve included 1,300 megawatts in our retail load growth forecast in 2030, with the remainder ramping up after that year."
  • The CEO emphasized customer and shareholder protections in LLPS tariffs, requiring "minimum monthly bills regardless of usage shortfalls that cover no less than 80% of their contracted capacity at a premium demand rate," as well as termination fees and collateral requirements.
  • Campbell highlighted a 4% dividend increase to an annualized $2.78, with the payout ratio expected to decline over time to 50% to 60%.
  • CFO Bryan Buckler stated, “For the full year 2025, Evergy delivered adjusted earnings of $894 million or $3.83 per share compared to $878 million or $3.81 per share for the same period last year.” Buckler added that "early indications in 2026 are strong in comparison to 2025, and we expect to return to normal residential load growth in 2026."

Outlook

  • Evergy projects a 2026 EPS guidance midpoint of $4.24. Management reiterated "annual adjusted growth of 6% to 8% plus through 2030 off of our 2026 midpoint guidance of $4.24 per share."
  • Buckler explained the company expects "EPS growth to exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 and through 2030" and "a retail load growth CAGR of approximately 6% through 2030."
  • The dividend payout ratio is targeted to reach 50% to 60% in the latter half of the plan period.
  • No equity issuances are planned in 2030, with the annual equity need from 2026 to 2029 forecasted at $700 million to $900 million.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted earnings for 2025 were $894 million or $3.83 per share, compared to $878 million or $3.81 per share for 2024.
  • Buckler detailed that 0.3% growth in weather-normalized demand drove a $0.04 per share margin increase. Recovery of regulated investments contributed $0.56 in EPS, while higher operation and maintenance, depreciation, and interest expense drove a $0.43 decrease.
  • Dilution from convertible notes led to a $0.05 decrease in EPS for 2025.
  • The company’s rolling 5-year investment plan totals approximately $21.6 billion from 2026 to 2030, a $4.1 billion increase over the prior plan, with rate base growth forecast at 11.5% annualized through 2030.

Q&A

  • Stephen D'Ambrisi, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about the absence of planned equity issuances in 2030 and future equity needs. Buckler responded that "no need for equity in 2030 because our FFO just greatly improves each year," but indicated more ESAs could require reevaluation of capital needs.
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies LLC: Queried about the EPS CAGR. Campbell clarified, "The overall formulation, 6% to 8% plus... '26, '27 in the bottom half of the 6% to 8% range. For that, we expect to accelerate to exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 then through the 2030 time frame."
  • Michael Sullivan, Wolfe Research: Asked about incremental load growth and CapEx. Buckler said the company uses "50-50 on debt equity funding of incremental capital," and Campbell noted that new ESAs would extend growth into the 2030s.
  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg Thalmann: Inquired about industrial rates and cancellation provisions. Charles Caisley reported, "Our typical range is in the vicinity of $0.06 to $0.07 a kilowatt hour." Campbell explained, "the LLPS provisions are strong" and offer protections through premium rates and infrastructure recovery.
  • Anthony Crowdell, Mizuho: Asked about FFO to debt ratio. Buckler responded that "FFO to debt 2025 was right around that 14% area as well."
  • Ryan Levine, Citi: Sought information on alternative capital sources. Campbell responded, "the plan that we announced today is through the traditional financing mechanisms... For that next tier in our pipeline, we're absolutely open to and will be considering different paths."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently focused on the sustainability of growth, equity funding, contract specifics, and credit metrics, with a tone that was largely positive but occasionally probing for more detail on risk protections and financial flexibility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, with Campbell stating "we are committed to delivering against our objective of sound financial execution" and Buckler emphasizing "we have high confidence in this 2026 guidance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted to more specific questions on capital needs and contract protections, while management’s tone grew more optimistic with the announcement of transformative data center agreements and raised growth targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a substantially higher EPS growth target (6% to 8% plus) and a step-up in projected rate base and capital investments, compared to more cautious forward-looking statements in the previous quarter.
  • Management updated on the successful signing of four major data center deals, transitioning from pipeline discussions to binding agreements, which was not present in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on growth, capital requirements, and regulatory issues, but the latest call featured more detail on the magnitude and timing of equity needs and protections in customer agreements.
  • Management’s confidence increased, with repeated emphasis on the transformative nature of the new data center contracts and their impact on future growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited negative impacts from weather and weak industrial demand in 2025, though early 2026 trends were described as strong.
  • The company embedded recent industrial load weakness in 2026 modeling and expects improvement as projects like Panasonic ramp up production.
  • Protections in LLPS tariffs and ESAs, such as minimum bills, creditworthiness standards, and termination fees, were discussed as mitigation against customer underperformance or cancellation.

Final Takeaway

Evergy’s leadership highlighted a transformative period ahead, setting a new long-term adjusted EPS growth target of 6% to 8% plus through 2030, underpinned by major data center agreements and a significant capital investment plan. The company emphasized robust protections in new large customer agreements and strong early signs for 2026 demand, projecting accelerating growth and improved financial flexibility while maintaining a focus on affordability and reliability for existing customers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Delinquency rates deteriorate, charge-offs mixed: January Credit Pulse
data.description:
Credit card trusts saw  delinquency rates rise modestly in January for each of the seven banks tracked by Seeking Alpha in its monthly roundup, while net charge-off rates were a mixed lot. Taking a long view, though, the delinquency and charge-off numbers still appear stronger than the metrics from January 2020, before the pandemic roiled the economy.

Either consumers paid down some debt or credit card lenders pulled back on lending last month. Total loans from the seven trusts were $521.4B, down 2.3% from December's total. 

The average delinquency rate in January increased to 2.81% from 2.75% in December and from 2.58% a year ago.

The average net charge-off rate of 3.53% rose from 3.27% in the prior month and dropped from 4.11% in January 2025. Bread Holdings (BFH), though, can skew the average because its net charge-off rate is significantly higher than the others. Excluding Bread, the average net charge-off rate was 2.94%, down from 3.13% in December and 3.50% in January 2025.

American Express (AXP), Synchrony Financial (SYF), Citigroup (C), and Bread Financial (BFH) saw their net charge-off rates decline month-over-month, while Capital One (COF), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC) saw net charge-off rates rise.

Total delinquencies for J.P. Morgan's Trust Tracker Index rose by 5 basis points M/M to 1.28%, in line with J.P. Morgan analyst Richard Shane's expectations. Net charge-offs for the index improved by 11 bps M/M to 1.84%, "modestly outperforming JPMe of 1.92%," the analyst noted.
